content.problemStatement:
There are NNN people on a street (numbered 111 through NNN). For simplicity, we'll view them as points on a line. For each valid iii, the position of the iii-th person is XiX_iXi​.

It turns out that exactly one of these people is infected with the virus COVID-19, but we do not know which one. The virus will spread from an infected person to a non-infected person whenever the distance between them is at most 222. If we wait long enough, a specific set of people (depending on the person that was infected initially) will become infected; let's call the size of this set the final number of infected people.

Your task is to find the smallest and largest value of the final number of infected people, i.e. this number in the best and in the worst possible scenario.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: first line contains an integer N (1 ≤ N ≤ 1000) – the number of people. Second line contains N distinct integers X1, X2, …, XN (0 ≤ Xi ≤ 10^9) in strictly increasing order, the positions of the people on the line.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line with two integers separated by a space: the minimum possible final number of infected people and the maximum possible final number of infected people.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Case 1: distance 3 > 2, each person stays isolated → component sizes 1 and 1. Case 2: every adjacent distance is exactly 2, all 3 people belong to one component. Case 3: positions form components {1,2} (size 2) and {5,6,7} (size 3). Minimum size is 2, maximum size is 3.
Positions 0‑1 are within distance 2 → component size 2. Positions 4‑5 form another component of size 2. Both components have the same size, so min = max = 2.
All consecutive gaps are exactly 2, therefore the whole set is a single connected component of size 6.

solution.approach: The virus can spread only between people whose distance is at most 2. Because the positions are sorted, the whole set splits into maximal contiguous groups where every adjacent gap ≤ 2. Each such group forms a connected component and will be completely infected if any member of the group is the initial carrier. Therefore the final infected size for a starting person equals the size of the component containing that person. The answer is simply the smallest and the largest component sizes. We scan the array once, keep a running count of the current component length, and update global minima and maxima whenever a gap > 2 ends the component.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(total N) over all test cases.
